content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 270 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2PE_002_page_001.jpg

1. But there arose also false prophets among the people, just as there will be false teachers among you also, who will secretly bring in destructive heresies, even denying the Master who bought them, bringing upon themselves swift destruction.

2. And many will follow their licentious ways, on account of whom the way of truth will be blasphemed.

3. And in covetousness they will exploit you with fabricated words; for whom the judgment from of old is not idle, and their destruction does not slumber.

4. For if God did not spare the angels who sinned, but cast them into Tartarus and delivered them to chains of darkness, to be kept for judgment;

5. and did not spare the ancient world, but preserved Noah, the eighth person, a herald of righteousness, when he brought a flood upon the world of the ungodly;

6. and condemned the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah to destruction, reducing them to ashes, having made them an example of what is coming upon the ungodly;

7. and rescued righteous Lot, who was distressed by the conduct of the lawless in their licentiousness —

8. for by what he saw and heard, that righteous man, dwelling among them day after day, was tormented in his righteous soul by their lawless deeds —

9. the Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trial, and to keep the unrighteous under punishment for the day of judgment,

10. and especially those who go after the flesh in the lust of defilement and despise authority. Bold and self-willed, they do not tremble when they blaspheme the glorious ones,

11. whereas angels, though greater in strength and power, do not bring a blasphemous judgment against them before the Lord.

12. But these, like irrational animals, creatures of instinct, born for capture and destruction, blaspheming in matters of which they are ignorant, will in their destruction also be destroyed,

13. suffering wrong as the wages of wrongdoing. They count it a pleasure to revel in the daytime. They are stains and blemishes, reveling in their deceptions while they feast together with you.

14. Having eyes full of adultery and unceasing from sin, they entice unstable souls. They have a heart trained in covetousness — accursed children!

15. Forsaking the straight way, they went astray, having followed the way of Balaam the son of Bosor, who loved the wages of unrighteousness,

16. but received a rebuke for his own transgression: a speechless donkey, speaking with a human voice, restrained the madness of the prophet.

17. These are waterless springs and mists driven by a storm, for whom the gloom of darkness has been reserved.

18. For speaking arrogant words of vanity, they entice by fleshly desires, by licentiousness, those who are barely escaping from those who live in error,

19. promising them freedom while they themselves are slaves of corruption; for by whatever a person is overcome, by this he is enslaved.

20. For if, having escaped the defilements of the world through the knowledge of the Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, they are again entangled and overcome by them, the last state has become worse for them than the first.

21. For it would have been better for them not to have known the way of righteousness than, having known it, to turn back from the holy commandment delivered to them.

22. It has happened to them according to the true proverb: "A dog returns to its own vomit," and, "A sow, having washed, returns to wallowing in the mire."

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Destructive heresies" (v.1):** The Greek αἱρέσεις ἀπωλείας denotes "factions/choices of destruction." In the formal register, "heresies" preserves the theological weight the term already carries by the time of 2 Peter, though the root sense is "chosen divisions." The Sinaiticus reading preserves the standard text here. The phrase "the Master who bought them" (τὸν ἀγοράσαντα αὐτοὺς δεσπότην) uses the marketplace metaphor of redemption — δεσπότης ("Master") rather than κύριος ("Lord") underscores sovereign ownership.

2. **"Cast into Tartarus" (v.4):** The verb ταρταρώσας is a hapax legomenon in the entire New Testament — it occurs only here. Tartarus is borrowed from Greek mythology as the subterranean prison of the Titans; the author repurposes it for the place of angelic confinement. Sinaiticus reads σειραῖς ζόφου ("chains of darkness") rather than the variant σιροῖς ("pits"), which is found in some later manuscripts.

3. **"Noah, the eighth person" (v.5):** The phrase ὄγδοον Νῶε means "Noah as the eighth" — that is, one of eight persons preserved (Noah, his wife, three sons, and their wives). This is an idiom indicating he was counted among a group of eight, not that he was the eighth in sequence.

4. **"Reducing to ashes" (v.6):** The verb τεφρώσας (from τέφρα, "ashes") appears only here in the New Testament. Sinaiticus includes both Sodom and Gomorrah by name. The word καταστροφῇ ("destruction/catastrophe") is the etymological source of English "catastrophe" and carries the spatial sense of an overturning.

5. **"Balaam the son of Bosor" (v.15):** Sinaiticus reads Βοσορ rather than the Old Testament's Beor (בְּעוֹר). This variant spelling is characteristic of the Petrine epistles and may reflect a popular etymology or phonetic shift in the tradition. Balaam serves as the archetype of the prophet corrupted by financial gain.

6. **"Waterless springs" (v.17):** The dual metaphor — πηγαὶ ἄνυδροι ("waterless springs") and ὁμίχλαι ὑπὸ λαίλαπος ἐλαυνόμεναι ("mists driven by a storm") — depicts the false teachers as promising refreshment but delivering nothing. Sinaiticus reads ὁμίχλαι ("mists") where some manuscripts have νεφέλαι ("clouds").

7. **"A dog returns to its own vomit" (v.22):** The first proverb is drawn from Proverbs 26:11 (LXX). The second, about the sow, is not found in the Hebrew Bible or Septuagint and likely comes from a popular folk saying, possibly with connections to the Story of Ahiqar or other ancient Near Eastern wisdom traditions. Together they form a grim inclusio for the chapter: apostasy is a return to the filth one was delivered from.

8. **Sinaiticus textual notes:** The manuscript is generally well-preserved for this chapter. Notable readings include ἀσεβέσιν in v.6 (the ungodly), the spelling Βοσορ in v.15, and ὁμίχλαι in v.17. The scribe's hand is consistent throughout the four columns on page 270 with no visible corrections in this chapter.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
